 // Hide all divs initially
 $('.hover-div-1, .hover-div-2, .hover-div-3, .hover-div-4, .hover-div-5').hide();
 // Declare a variable to track the currently visible div
 let currentDiv = null;
 // Function to show the div and hide others
 function showDiv(targetDiv) {
 // If there is a current div, fade it out immediately
 if (currentDiv && currentDiv !== targetDiv) {
 currentDiv.stop(true, true).fadeOut(300); // Stop previous fade-out and fade out immediately
 } 
 // Fade in the target div
 targetDiv.stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 currentDiv = targetDiv; // Set the current div to the new one
 }
 // For Text 1
 $('.text-item-1').mouseenter(function() {
 showDiv($('.hover-div-1'));
 });
 // For Text 2
 $('.text-item-2').mouseenter(function() {
 showDiv($('.hover-div-2'));
 });
 // For Text 3
 $('.text-item-3').mouseenter(function() {
 showDiv($('.hover-div-3'));
 });
 // For Text 4
 $('.text-item-4').mouseenter(function() {
 showDiv($('.hover-div-4'));
 });
 // For Text 5
 $('.text-item-5').mouseenter(function() {
 showDiv($('.hover-div-5'));
 });
 // Keep the div open while hovering over the text or the div
 $('.text-container .text-item-1, .hover-div-1').hover(function() {
 $('.hover-div-1').stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 });
 $('.text-container .text-item-2, .hover-div-2').hover(function() {
 $('.hover-div-2').stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 });
 $('.text-container .text-item-3, .hover-div-3').hover(function() {
 $('.hover-div-3').stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 });
 $('.text-container .text-item-4, .hover-div-4').hover(function() {
 $('.hover-div-4').stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 });
 $('.text-container .text-item-5, .hover-div-5').hover(function() {
 $('.hover-div-5').stop(true, true).fadeIn(300);
 });
 // Only fade out the div when you leave both the text and the hidden div and text-container
 function setFadeOutForDiv(divClass, textClass) {
 $(divClass + ', ' + textClass).mouseleave(function() {
 if (!$(divClass).is(':hover') && !$(textClass).is(':hover') && !$('.text-container').is(':hover')) {
 $(divClass).fadeOut(300);
 }
 });
 }
 // Set the fade out for all div-text combinations
 setFadeOutForDiv('.hover-div-1', '.text-item-1');
 setFadeOutForDiv('.hover-div-2', '.text-item-2');
 setFadeOutForDiv('.hover-div-3', '.text-item-3');
 setFadeOutForDiv('.hover-div-4', '.text-item-4');
 setFadeOutForDiv('.hover-div-5', '.text-item-5');
}); </script> <style> /* dual color headings */

What\u2019s Next After January\u2019s Record High","description":"In January, copper prices reached an all-time high on the London Metal Exchange, after breaking through $14,000 per tonne. The rally was driven by surging demand, a projected supply deficit and expectations of solid economic growth. But copper has since pared the gains, with forecasts suggesting the rally may have run out of road. While","articleBody":"In January, copper prices reached an all-time high on the London Metal Exchange, after breaking through $14,000 per tonne.        The rally was driven by surging demand, a projected supply deficit and expectations of solid economic growth.        But copper has since pared the gains, with forecasts suggesting the rally may have run out of road.        While the long-term outlook for copper remains bullish, analysts project there may only be limited upside potential this year.            Is Copper Worth Investing in? Near-term Headwinds Could Keep Prices Subdued    The long-term case for copper rests on a clear structural case, namely that demand for the metal is expected to rise, while supply struggles to keep up.        That deficit is expected to send copper as high as $15,000 per tonne by 2035, according to analysts at Goldman Sachs.        But in the near term, copper prices may remain subdued, with the market not expected to enter a deficit until 2029.        As a result, Goldman predicts that copper prices will average $10,710 on the LME in the first half of this year, with an improved outlook for 2027:        \u201cBeyond 2026, Goldman Sachs Research is bullish on copper prices. The team expects demand for the metal to overtake supply from 2029 onwards, pushing prices higher, which is expected to incentivise the development of new copper supply (mainly by extending the life of mines, but also through more scrap use) and also to curtail demand.\u201d        Analysts at JP Morgan are more bullish and forecast copper to average $12,500 in Q2 of this year, with demand for AI infrastructure identified as a key upside risk to the outlook.                In their most recent report, Gregory Shearer, head of base and precious metals strategy at the bank, said supply disruptions could help to keep prices elevated:        \u201cAll in all, we think these unique dynamics of disjointed inventory and acute supply disruptions tightening the copper market add up to a bullish set up for copper, and are enough to push prices above $12,000\/mt in the first half of 2026,\u201d        These projections are in line with the consensus forecasts, which show copper averaging $10,000 and $12,500 per tonne in 2026, with Fidelity analysts noting this may indicate that \u201ccopper may have already enjoyed its run, in the short term at least.\u201d            Does The Iran War Create A Buying Opportunity?    Most of the published price targets pre-date the onset of the Iran war, a conflict which threatens to spark a global downturn, and potentially, weigh on copper demand.        And yet, some analysts think the conflict may represent an opportunity to buy copper, after the outbreak of the war exerted selling pressure in the metals markets.        Speaking to Barron\u2019s, Kevin Smith, chief investment officer at Crescat Capital, said he believed the Iran pullback was \u2018temporary and an overreaction\u2019, which he said represented a buying opportunity for select copper mining stocks.        However, the war is a double-edged sword, insofar as a long war could dent copper demand in the medium term, and for this reason, some analysts see it as a risk factor.        Commenting this week, Grant Sporre, the global head of metals and mining at Bloomberg Intelligence, said while the long-term case for copper is clear, the Iran war is blowing a headwind:        \u201cWhile copper\u2019s long-term fundamentals remain intact, near-term pricing and margins are highly sensitive to energy-driven inflation and supply disruptions.\u201d.        Copper has long been seen as an economic indicator of health, and the metal shows a strong correlation with economic growth. A global downturn, sparked by persistent energy prices, could well relieve demand for copper and put downward pressure on prices.        Analysts are taking the threat of recession increasingly seriously, and a stagflation scenario remains on the table. Speaking to CNBC last week, Peter Boockvar, CIO at One Point BFG Wealth Partners, said the risk had people \u201creally worried\u201d about industrial metals.            The Long-term Case for Copper    In a March 2026 report, published in the Energy Research & Social Science Journal, a group of academics argued that copper is essential to the modern economy, and yet \u201ccopper is unlikely to be mined fast enough to meet all these needs in the short to medium term.\u201d        The report argued that the price of copper may \u201crise significantly and provide robust price signals for a significant number of known copper deposits to be developed into mines.\u201d        Expounding on the report with his co-author, Morgan Bazilian, Adam Simon, of the University of Michigan, said in a follow-up article for The Conversation, that long lead times to bring new supply online made a supply deficit look increasingly likely:        \u201cThe only other way to get more copper is to mine more of it. But building a new mine can take 20 to 30 years \u2013 a period during which investors are spending money but not yet getting returns, and a time when costs can rise significantly from preliminary estimates.        \u201cIf industrial and economic growth is to stay on track in the 2030s, new mines would need to be in the financing and permitting processes right now. But they aren\u2019t.\u201d        That\u2019s in line with the broader consensus on copper, which many analysts believe could make substantial gains through the 2030s.        In a March report published by the IEA, analysts identified the same supply-side challenges, which they said pointed toward a structurally tighter market in the coming years:        \u201cead times for new copper projects are long \u2013 around 17 years from discovery to production \u2013 and many major projects have recently experienced significant delays.                \u201cThese dynamics suggest a structurally tighter market for copper concentrate (raw materials for smelters), raising risks for supply security and underscoring the need for supply- and demand-side measures \u2013 from stimulating investment in new mines to advancing material efficiency, substitution and recycling.\u201d        The latest estimations from S&P Global put the copper supply gap at 24 per cent by 2040, as emerging technologies, defence and electrification drive \u201cunprecedented\u201d copper demand.                    Copper Price Forecast 2026: Near-term Looks Subdued While Long-term Case Holds    After coming off its January all-time high of over $14,000, analysts believe copper may remain relatively subdued for the remainder of the year.        Reuters \u2019 poll of metals analysts found a consensus copper price forecast for 2026 of $11,000, roughly in line with analysts at Goldman Sachs and JP Morgan, who see $10,710 in H1 and $12,500 in Q2, respectively.                However, the long-term case for copper rests on structural dynamics, which many analysts think will see copper prices rise through the 2030s.        The International Energy Agency said in March that copper could face a 30 per cent supply deficit by 2035, while S&P Global has warned of a 10 million tonne shortfall by 2040 as electrification, defence spending and AI push demand higher.","datePublished":"2026-04-07T09:29:25+02:00","dateModified":"2026-04-08T05:55:42+02:00","author":{"@type":"Person","name":"Ivan class="elementor-heading-title elementor-size-xl">Is Copper Going to Skyrocket? What’s Next After January’s Record High</h1></div></div></div></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-6593af33 e-flex e-con-boxed e-con e-parent" data-id="6593af33" data-element_type="container" data-e-type="container"><div class="e-con-inner"><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-74d0b183 e-con-full e-flex e-con e-child" data-id="74d0b183" data-element_type="container" data-e-type="container"><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-a044a74 e-con-full e-flex e-con e-child" data-id="a044a74" data-element_type="container" data-e-type="container"><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-0a03252 elementor-widget elementor-widget-image" data-id="0a03252" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="image.default"> <img loading="lazy" width="768" height="1024" src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/20180309_200433-210021-768x1024.jpg" class="attachment-large size-large wp-image-3811" alt="" srcset="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/20180309_200433-210021-768x1024.jpg 768w, https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/20180309_200433-210021-225x300.jpg 225w, https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/20180309_200433-210021.webp 1100w" sizes="(max-width: 768px) 100vw, 768px" /></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-380b8925 elementor-widget elementor-widget-heading" data-id="380b8925" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="heading.default"><p class="elementor-heading-title elementor-size-default">By</p></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-9b2b582 elementor-widget elementor-widget-text-editor" data-id="9b2b582" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="text-editor.default"><p>Mario Lagos</p></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-d3fcccd elementor-widget elementor-widget-text-editor" data-id="d3fcccd" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="text-editor.default"> April 7, 2026</div></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-e406ded elementor-widget elementor-widget-shortcode" data-id="e406ded" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="shortcode.default"><div class="elementor-shortcode"><div data-elementor-type="container" data-elementor-id="25359" class="elementor elementor-25359" data-elementor-post-type="elementor_library"></div></div></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-6a88a0bf animated-slow elementor-invisible elementor-widget elementor-widget-theme-post-content" data-id="6a88a0bf" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-settings="{"_animation":"fadeIn","_animation_delay":400}" data-widget_type="theme-post-content.default"> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">In January, copper prices reached an all-time high on the London Metal Exchange, after breaking through $14,000 per tonne.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">The rally was driven by surging demand, a projected supply deficit and expectations of solid economic growth.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">But copper has since pared the gains, with forecasts suggesting the rally may have run out of road.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">While the long-term outlook for copper remains bullish, analysts project there may only be limited upside potential this year.</p> <br> <br><h2 class="wp-block-heading">Is Copper Worth Investing in? Near-term Headwinds Could Keep Prices Subdued</h2><p class="wp-block-paragraph">The long-term case for copper rests on a clear structural case, namely that demand for the metal is expected to rise, while supply struggles to keep up.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">That deficit is expected to send <a href="https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/copper-prices-forecast-to-decline-from-record-highs-in-2026">copper as high as $15,000 per tonne by 2035</a>, according to analysts at Goldman Sachs.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">But in the near term, copper prices may remain subdued, with the market not expected to enter a deficit until 2029.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">As a result, Goldman predicts that copper prices will average $10,710 on the LME in the first half of this year, with an improved outlook for 2027:</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“Beyond 2026, Goldman Sachs Research is bullish on copper prices. The team expects demand for the metal to overtake supply from 2029 onwards, pushing prices higher, which is expected to incentivise the development of new copper supply (mainly by extending the life of mines, but also through more scrap use) and also to curtail demand.”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Analysts at JP Morgan are more bullish and <a href="https://www.jpmorgan.com/insights/global-research/commodities/copper-outlook">forecast copper to average $12,500 in Q2 of this year</a>, with demand for AI infrastructure identified as a key upside risk to the outlook.</p> <br><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-full"><img decoding="async" src="data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7" data-src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/GLOBAL-COPPER-DEMAND-BY-SECTOR.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26761 lazyload"/><noscript><img decoding="async" src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/GLOBAL-COPPER-DEMAND-BY-SECTOR.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26761 lazyload"/></noscript></figure> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">In their most recent report, Gregory Shearer, head of base and precious metals strategy at the bank, said supply disruptions could help to keep prices elevated:</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“All in all, we think these unique dynamics of disjointed inventory and acute supply disruptions tightening the copper market add up to a bullish set up for copper, and are enough to push prices above $12,000/mt in the first half of 2026,”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">These projections are in line with <a href="https://www.fidelity.co.uk/markets-insights/markets/global/why-is-the-copper-price-rising/#:~:text=What%20has%20happened%20to%20the,What's%20driving%20demand%20for%20copper?">the consensus forecasts</a>, which show copper averaging $10,000 and $12,500 per tonne in 2026, with Fidelity analysts noting this may indicate that “copper may have already enjoyed its run, in the short term at least.”</p> <br> <br><h2 class="wp-block-heading">Does The Iran War Create A Buying Opportunity?</h2><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Most of the published price targets pre-date the onset of the Iran war, a conflict which threatens to spark a global downturn, and potentially, weigh on copper demand.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">And yet, some analysts think the conflict may represent an opportunity to buy copper, after <a href="https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/copper">the outbreak of the war exerted selling pressure in the metals markets</a>.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Speaking to Barron’s, Kevin Smith, chief investment officer at Crescat Capital, said he believed the Iran pullback was ‘temporary and an overreaction’, which he said represented a buying opportunity for select copper mining stocks.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">However, the war is a double-edged sword, insofar as a long war could dent copper demand in the medium term, and for this reason, some analysts see it as a risk factor.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Commenting this week, Grant Sporre, the global head of metals and mining at Bloomberg Intelligence, said <a href="https://www.mining.com/prolonged-iran-war-would-hammer-top-copper-miners/">while the long-term case for copper is clear, the Iran war is blowing a headwind</a>:</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“While copper’s long-term fundamentals remain intact, near-term pricing and margins are highly sensitive to energy-driven inflation and supply disruptions.”.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.reuters.com/plus/copper-as-an-economic-indicator">Copper has long been seen as an economic indicator</a> of health, and the metal shows a strong correlation with economic growth. A global downturn, sparked by persistent energy prices, could well relieve demand for copper and put downward pressure on prices.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Analysts are taking the threat of recession increasingly seriously, and a stagflation scenario remains on the table. Speaking to CNBC last week, Peter Boockvar, CIO at One Point BFG Wealth Partners, said <a href="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/19/copper-joins-gold-in-broad-commodities-sell-off-theres-a-worrying-reason-behind-it.html">the risk had people “really worried” about industrial metals</a>.</p> <br> <br><h2 class="wp-block-heading">The Long-term Case for Copper</h2><p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2214629626000526?via%3Dihub">In a March 2026 report</a>, published in the Energy Research & Social Science Journal, a group of academics argued that copper is essential to the modern economy, and yet “copper is unlikely to be mined fast enough to meet all these needs in the short to medium term.”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">The report argued that the price of copper may “rise significantly and provide robust price signals for a significant number of known copper deposits to be developed into mines.”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Expounding on the report with his co-author, Morgan Bazilian, Adam Simon, of the University of Michigan, said in a follow-up article for The Conversation, that long lead times to bring new supply online made a supply deficit look increasingly likely:</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“The only other way to get more copper is to mine more of it. But building a new mine can take 20 to 30 years – a period during which investors are spending money but not yet getting returns, and a time when costs can rise significantly from preliminary estimates.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“If industrial and economic growth is to stay on track in the 2030s, new mines would need to be in the financing and permitting processes right now. But they aren’t.”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">That’s in line with <a href="https://www.devere-group.com/copper-price-forecast-2026-analysts-issue-bullish-outlook-amid-supply-shortage/">the broader consensus on copper</a>, which many analysts believe could make substantial gains through the 2030s.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">In a March report published by the IEA, analysts identified the same supply-side challenges, which they said pointed toward a structurally tighter market in the coming years:</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“[L]ead times for new copper projects are long – around 17 years from discovery to production – and many major projects have recently experienced significant delays.</p> <br><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-full"><img decoding="async" src="data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7" data-src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/COPPER-17-30-YEARS.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26753 lazyload"/><noscript><img decoding="async" src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/COPPER-17-30-YEARS.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26753 lazyload"/></noscript></figure> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">“These dynamics suggest a structurally tighter market for copper concentrate (raw materials for smelters), raising risks for supply security and underscoring the need for supply- and demand-side measures – from stimulating investment in new mines to advancing material efficiency, substitution and recycling.”</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">The latest <a href="https://www.spglobal.com/energy/en/news-research/latest-news/metals/010826-copper-supply-gap-to-widen-24-by-2040-as-electrification-accelerates-study">estimations from S&P Global put the copper supply gap at 24 per cent by 2040</a>, as emerging technologies, defence and electrification drive “unprecedented” copper demand.</p> <br><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-full"><img decoding="async" src="data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7" data-src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/30-GAP.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26756 lazyload"/><noscript><img decoding="async" src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/30-GAP.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26756 lazyload"/></noscript></figure> <br> <br><h2 class="wp-block-heading">Copper Price Forecast 2026: Near-term Looks Subdued While Long-term Case Holds</h2><p class="wp-block-paragraph">After coming off its January all-time high of over $14,000, analysts believe copper may remain relatively subdued for the remainder of the year.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">Reuters ’ poll of <a href="https://www.reuters.com/world/asia-pacific/copper-forecasts-jump-above-11000-first-time-analysts-wary-demand-worries-2026-01-29/">metals analysts found a consensus copper price forecast for 2026 of $11,000</a>, roughly in line with analysts at Goldman Sachs and JP Morgan, who see $10,710 in H1 and $12,500 in Q2, respectively.</p> <br><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-full"><img decoding="async" src="data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7" data-src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/COPPER-PRICE-FORECAST.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26763 lazyload"/><noscript><img decoding="async" src="https://www.devere-group.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/COPPER-PRICE-FORECAST.webp" alt="" class="wp-image-26763 lazyload"/></noscript></figure> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">However, the long-term case for copper rests on structural dynamics, which many analysts think will see copper prices rise through the 2030s.</p> <br><p class="wp-block-paragraph">The International Energy Agency said in March that copper could face a 30 per cent supply deficit by 2035, while S&P Global has warned of a 10 million tonne shortfall by 2040 as electrification, defence spending and AI push demand higher.</p> <br></div></div><div class="elementor-element elementor-element-5cf69f1a e-con-full e-flex e-con e-child" })(jQuery); </script> <script> (() => {
 const OLD_FULL = "deVere Group India Investment Advisors Private Limited";
 const NEW_FULL = "deVere Group India Investment Advisers Private Limited";
 const TARGET_ID = "114";
 const norm = s => (s || "").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
 function replaceInTextNodes(root, pairs) {
 const walker = document.createTreeWalker(root, NodeFilter.SHOW_TEXT, null);
 let changed = false;
 while (walker.nextNode()) {
 const node = walker.currentNode;
 let v = node.nodeValue, nv = v;
 for (const [from, to] of pairs) {
 nv = nv.split(from).join(to);
 }
 if (nv !== v) {
 node.nodeValue = nv;
 changed = true;
 }
 }
 return changed;
 }
 function updateTargetLi() {
 const li = document.querySelector(`#location-list li[data-company-id="${TARGET_ID}"]`);
 if (!li) return;
 const labelEl = li.querySelector('a, span, p, h1,h2,h3,h4,h5,h6') || li;
 if (norm(labelEl.textContent) === norm(OLD_FULL)) {
 labelEl.textContent = NEW_FULL;
 } else {
 // Fallbacks para casos com tags internas
 replaceInTextNodes(li, [
 [OLD_FULL, NEW_FULL],
 ["Advisors Private Limited", "Advisers Private Limited"],
 ["Advisors", "Advisers"],
 ]);
 }
 // Mantém atributos coerentes, se existirem
 ["title", "aria-label"].forEach(attr => {
 const val = li.getAttribute(attr);
 if (val && val.includes("Advisors")) {
 li.setAttribute(attr, val.replaceAll("Advisors", "Advisers"));
 }
 });
 }
 function updateGMapH3() {
 document.querySelectorAll(".gm-style-iw-d h3").forEach(h3 => {
 if (norm(h3.textContent) === norm(OLD_FULL)) {
 h3.textContent = NEW_FULL;
 } else if (h3.textContent.includes("Advisors")) {
 h3.textContent = h3.textContent.replaceAll("Advisors", "Advisers");
 }
 });
 }
 function run() {
 updateTargetLi();
 updateGMapH3();
 }
 // Executa de início
 if (document.readyState === "loading") {
 document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", run, { once: true });
 } else {
 run();
 }
 // Observa atualizações dinâmicas (Maps/SPA/AJAX)
 const mo = new MutationObserver(muts => {
 for (const m of muts) {
 if (m.type === "childList") {
 for (const n of m.addedNodes) {
 if (!(n instanceof Element)) continue;
 if (
 n.matches?.('#location-list, .gm-style-iw-d') ||
 n.querySelector?.('#location-list, .gm-style-iw-d')
 ) run();
 }
 } else if (m.type === "characterData") {
 const el = m.target.parentNode;
 if (el && el.nodeType === 1 && (el.closest('#location-list') || el.closest('.gm-style-iw-d'))) {
 run();
 }
 }
 }
 });
 mo.observe(document.body, { subtree: true, childList: true, characterData: true });
})(); </script></body></html>
